text.title: API Documentation Required Sections
text.summary: Mandatory sections and their minimum content for any public-facing API reference.

rule.statement: Every API reference must include: Overview, Authentication, Quick Start (with copy-paste curl), Endpoints, Error Codes table, and Changelog.
rule.rationale: Developers evaluate APIs in under 5 minutes. Missing any of these six sections causes abandonment before integration begins.

rule.statement: Quick Start must include a working curl command that produces a visible result with no setup beyond an API key.
rule.rationale: A runnable first call is the fastest proof of value. SDKs and full docs can wait; a curl example cannot.

rule.statement: Error Codes table must include: code, meaning, and a resolution hint for every non-2xx response the API can return.
rule.rationale: Clients debug by error code. An error code with no resolution hint requires reading source code or filing a support ticket.

rule.statement: Changelog must record breaking changes, new endpoints, and bug fixes per version with a date.
rule.rationale: Consumers must know when to update their integrations. Undocumented breaking changes cause production outages.

rule.statement: Provide code examples in at least two languages (curl + one SDK); keep them synchronized with tests so they do not go stale.
rule.rationale: Most consumers copy-paste the example closest to their stack. Stale examples that do not work destroy trust faster than missing docs.

rule.statement: Include rate limit information (requests per window, headers returned, behavior on breach) in the Authentication or Overview section.
rule.rationale: Clients that hit rate limits without prior knowledge implement incorrect retry logic, causing cascading failures.

text.reference: Markdown template for a complete API reference page
